F arn P olrrrcer- P RACTICEs C ovuls s IoN
428 J Street r Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
September 12,2006
Rod Diridon, Jr.
Santa Claru City Clerk
1500 Warburton Avenue
Santa Clara, California 95050
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-06-173
Dear Mr. Diridon:
This is in response to your request for advice concerning your duties as filing
officer for campaign statements filed pursuant to the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTTONS
1. Regarding campaign filings, in what cases is "none" an appropriate
designation when disclosing a contributor's occupation and employer?
2. Under what circumstances should you request an amended Form 460
submitted with'"none" under occupation and employer and under what circumstances
should you not?
CONCLUSION
1. When a contributor has neither an occupation nor an employer, "none" in the
box requiring that information conveys that information and is acceptable.
2. A filer's use of the word "none" will not require an amendment when used to
describe the contributor's status as someone with no employer and no occupation.
I Gorern*e.rt Code sections 81000-91()14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Re,:ulations.


File No.A-06-173
Page No. 2
FACTS
As city clerk for the City of Santa Clara, you perform the duties of the filing
officer for campaign statements filed by city candidates and committees. You have
requested advice about your filing officer duties related to these requirements.
ANALYSIS
Under the Act, candidates and committees must file periodic statements
disclosing contributions received and expenditures made in connection with election
activities. (Section 84100, et seq.) For campaign statements filed by city candidates and
their committees, the city clerk is the filing officer. (Section 84215.) Section 81010
imposes the following duties on filing officers:
"(a) Supply the necessary forms and manuals
prescribed by the Commission;
(b) Determine whether required documents have
been filed and, if so, whether they conforrn on their face
with the requirements of this title;
(c) Notify promptly all persons and known
committees who have failed to file a report or staternent in
the form and at the time required by this title;
(d) Report apparent violations of this title to the
appropriate agencies; and
(e) Compile and maintain a current list of all
reports and statements filed with this office."
Your question pertains to subdivision (b) of section 81010, which regards your
role in determining whether the documents filed with your offices by campaign
committees conform on their face with the requirements of the Act. Specifically, you are
concerned with filers meeting the requirement that, for each contributor of $100 or more,
a committee must disclose the contributor's niune, address, and, if the contributor is an
individual, his or her occupation and employer. (Section 84211(f).) You have received
several Forms 460 that have been filed with the term "none" in the space provided for a
description of the contributor's occupation and employer.
Section 81004.5 provides the general authority to amend a statement by
establishingthat"La)ny report or statement filed pursuant to this title may be amended by
the filer at any time. Amending an incorrect or incomplete report or statement may be
considered as evidence of good faith." The FPPC has consistently advised that when a
filing officer discovers during his or her review of campaign statements that a candidate
or committee has filed an incorrect or incomplete statement, an amendment must be


File No.A-06-173
Page No. 3
requested.2 (D aniels -Meade Advice Letter,No. A-0 1 -2 8 5 ; Tucker Advice Letter,No. A-
97-176.) To determine whether the filers using the term "none" are incompletely
disclosing information regarding the contributor, we turn to the Webster's New World
Dictionary, Third Edition, which provides the following definitions of "employer" as
"one who employs; especially a person, business firm, etc. that hires one or more persons
to work for wages or salary" and of "occupation" as "one's trade, profession, or
business." Pursuant to these definitions, there are sifuations where a contributor has
neither employer nor occupation and in these situations, the filer may use the word
"none" to establish this fact. This would be the case where a contributor is a homemaker,
retired or is simply not employed (and is not self-employed).
In answer to your question regarding under what circumstances should you
request an amended Form 460 when one has been submitted with ohone" in the space for
the contributor's occupation and employer, we return agunto the language of section
81010(b), which establishes that, as a filing officer, you must determine whether required
documents "conform on their face with the requirements of this title." Although
"conform on their face" is not defined in the Act, we have interpreted it to mean that the
filing officer reviews the forms for errors or omissions. A filer's use of the word "none,,,
as determined above, is not an error or omission when used to describe the contributor's
status as someone with no employer and no occupation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
,.14T-Zr
By: Adrianne Korchmaro s C-Zr.)
Political Reform Consultant II
Technical Assistance Division
AK:rd
I:\Adviceltrs\06-173
2 Pursuant to regulation 18110(c), however, amendments need not be requested when errors or
omissions are minor or do not recur tfuoughout the campaign statement and do not result in omission of the
amount of an individual contribution or expenditure. (Daniels-Meade, supra; Tucker, supra.) In addition,
filing officers are not required to seek or obtain information to verifu entries on a campaign statement.
(Regulation 1 8 I 10(bxlXA).)

describe the contributor's status as someone with no employer and no occupation.
